responses: Hello! Welcome to the Secure Coding Guide. How can I help you today?
Hi there! Ready to learn how to protect your code?
Greetings! What security topic should we discuss today?

patterns: Why should I write secure code?
responses: It ensures compliance with international security standards like OWASP and ISO.
Secure coding minimizes the attack surface of your application by design.
It protects your company's reputation and prevents legal issues caused by data leaks.
Implementing security early in the development lifecycle (Shift Left) is much cheaper than fixing bugs after deployment.
It helps in preventing common threats like Broken Access Control and Sensitive Data Exposure.
To prevent SQL Injection, always use Prepared Statements and Parameterized Queries instead of concatenating strings.
Never trust user input; use ORMs or secure database libraries to block SQLi attacks.
SQL Injection occurs when an attacker inserts malicious SQL code into a query, tricking the database into executing unintended commands.
Always implement the 'Principle of Least Privilege' for your database users to limit the potential damage of an attack.
Use Input Validation to ensure that the data entered by the user matches the expected format before it is processed.
Modern ORMs like SQLAlchemy or Sequelize provide built-in protection against SQLi by automatically parameterizing queries.

patterns: What can an attacker do with XSS?
responses: To prevent XSS, you must always perform Output Encoding and Input Validation for all user-provided data.
Sanitize all inputs before rendering them in the browser to block malicious scripts.
XSS occurs when an attacker injects malicious scripts into web pages to steal cookies or sensitive user data.
Use a Content Security Policy (CSP) as a powerful defense layer to control which scripts are allowed to execute in your web application.
Stored XSS is especially dangerous because the malicious script is saved on the server and served to every user who visits the page.
Always set the 'HttpOnly' flag on sensitive cookies so they cannot be accessed by malicious scripts even if an XSS vulnerability exists.
Injection vulnerabilities occur when untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query.
The most common types are SQL Injection, Command Injection, LDAP Injection, and Cross-Site Scripting (XSS).
To prevent injection, you must always separate data from commands. Use safe APIs, parameterized queries, and validate all inputs.
In Command Injection, an attacker tries to execute arbitrary operating system (OS) commands via a vulnerable application.
The golden rule to stop Injection: Never trust user input. Always sanitize and use allow-lists for expected values.
CTF (Capture The Flag) is a cybersecurity competition where participants solve technical challenges to find a hidden string called a 'Flag'.
CTFs cover various fields like Web Hacking, Reverse Engineering, Cryptography, and Forensics.
To start, I recommend websites like 'picoCTF' for absolute beginners and 'Hack The Box' or 'TryHackMe' for more advanced practice.
Practicing CTFs is the best way to understand how attackers think, which helps you write more secure code in your projects.
There are two main types of CTF: Jeopardy-style (solving tasks) and Attack-Defense (protecting your server while attacking others).
Join platforms like CTFtime.org to keep track of global competitions and join a team to learn faster.

patterns: What is the golden rule of security?
The concept of trust in cyber security
responses: The golden rule of cybersecurity is: 'Never Trust, Always Verify'. Treat every piece of data coming from a user as potentially malicious.
Zero Trust means that your application should not automatically trust anything inside or outside its perimeters; it must verify everything before granting access.
Trusting user input blindly is the root cause of vulnerabilities like SQL Injection and XSS. Always validate, sanitize, and filter data.
Instead of trusting data, use 'Allow-lists' to define exactly what is permitted (e.g., only letters, only numbers, or a specific length).
Security starts by assuming the user might be an attacker. By removing 'trust' from the equation, you build much more resilient systems.
CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is an attack that forces an authenticated user to execute unwanted actions on a web application they are currently logged into.
Unlike XSS, which aims to steal data, CSRF aims to perform state-changing requests like changing passwords or transferring funds without the user's consent.
The best defense against CSRF is using 'Anti-CSRF Tokens'—unique, secret, and unpredictable values generated by the server for each user session.
Always use the 'SameSite' cookie attribute (Strict or Lax) to prevent the browser from sending cookies with cross-site requests.
For sensitive actions, require an additional layer of security like re-authentication (entering the password again) or a Captcha.
A successful CSRF attack targets functionality that causes a state change on the server, such as updating profile details or deleting data.
API Security focuses on protecting the application programming interfaces (APIs) from attacks and unauthorized access.
Always use Token-based authentication like JWT (JSON Web Tokens) or OAuth2 to ensure that only authorized users can access your endpoints.
Implement 'Rate Limiting' to prevent DoS attacks and brute-force attempts by limiting the number of requests a user can make in a certain time.
Always use HTTPS to encrypt data in transit between the client and the API server to prevent Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks.
Validate and Sanitize all input data at the API level, even if you already did it on the frontend. Never trust the client!
Avoid exposing sensitive data in API responses. Only return the fields that are absolutely necessary for the frontend to display.

patterns: How to audit code for security?
How to find vulnerabilities in my app?
responses: Security Auditing is a systematic evaluation of an application's security to ensure it meets safety standards and follows best practices.
Auditing involves two main methods: SAST (Static Analysis), which scans the source code, and DAST (Dynamic Analysis), which tests the app while it is running.
A manual code review is a critical part of auditing, where developers look for logic flaws that automated tools might miss.
Regular auditing helps identify vulnerabilities early in the development lifecycle, reducing the risk of a data breach.
To perform a good audit, use the OWASP Testing Guide as a checklist to cover everything from authentication to data encryption.
Security auditing is not a one-time task; it should be part of your Continuous Integration (CI/CD) pipeline to ensure every update is safe.

patterns: What is Salt in password hashing?
How to use BCrypt in Python?
responses: BCrypt is a password hashing function designed to be slow and secure, making it extremely difficult for attackers to crack passwords using brute force.
Unlike encryption, hashing is a one-way process. You cannot 'decrypt' a BCrypt hash back into the original password.
BCrypt automatically handles 'Salting'—adding random data to each password before hashing—which prevents attackers from using Rainbow Tables to find matches.
The 'Work Factor' or 'Cost' in BCrypt allows you to increase the time it takes to hash a password, staying ahead of faster computer hardware.
In Python, you can use the 'bcrypt' library to hash passwords with: bcrypt.hashpw(password, bcrypt.gensalt()).
Never use MD5 or SHA1 for passwords! Always use BCrypt, Argon2, or Scrypt for modern and secure password storage.

patterns: How to protect user data in DB?
responses: Database security involves protecting the database software, the data it holds, and the network it sits on from unauthorized access.
Always use the 'Principle of Least Privilege': The database user for your app should only have permission to SELECT, INSERT, and UPDATE, never to DROP or ALTER tables.
Encrypt sensitive data 'at rest' (in the DB) and 'in transit' (using TLS/SSL). Even if an attacker steals the database file, they won't be able to read the data.
Disable all unnecessary features and services on your database server to reduce the attack surface.
Never use the 'root' or 'admin' account for your web application's daily database operations.
Enable database logging and auditing to track who accessed what data and when, which helps in identifying suspicious activities early.

patterns: What is Identity and Access Management?
How to store API keys safely?
responses: Security Management involves defining and managing the roles and access privileges of individual network users.
Use Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to assign permissions to roles (like Admin, Editor, Viewer) rather than to individual users.
Secrets Management is crucial: Never hardcode API keys or database passwords in your source code. Use Environment Variables or tools like HashiCorp Vault.
Implement Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) to add an extra layer of security beyond just passwords.
Regularly perform User Access Reviews to ensure that people only have the access they currently need for their jobs (Least Privilege).
A centralized Management system helps in tracking user activities and revoking access instantly if a security breach is suspected.

patterns: Why is PoLP important in security?
responses: The Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP) means giving a user, account, or process only the minimum permissions necessary to perform its intended function.
PoLP is critical because it limits the 'attack surface'. If an account is compromised, the attacker can only do what that specific account was allowed to do.
In web development, your database user should only have SELECT/INSERT permissions, not DROP or DELETE, unless absolutely necessary.
Applying PoLP helps prevent 'Privilege Escalation' and protects sensitive data from being accessed by unauthorized users.
Regularly audit your system to revoke unnecessary permissions. If someone doesn't need access to the admin panel, they shouldn't have it.

patterns: How to build secure software from scratch?
responses: SDLC (Software Development Life Cycle) is a process used by software industry to design, develop, and test high-quality softwares.
The main phases of SDLC are: Planning, Analysis, Design, Implementation (Coding), Testing, Deployment, and Maintenance.
In a Secure SDLC, we add security at every stage: Risk Assessment during planning, Threat Modeling during design, and Static Analysis during coding.
The 'Shift Left' approach in SDLC means moving security testing to the earliest possible point in the development process to save time and costs.
The Testing phase is where we perform DAST and Penetration Testing to ensure the application is resilient against real-world attacks.
Maintenance is the final phase where we monitor the app for new vulnerabilities and apply security patches regularly.

patterns: Difference between Information Security and Cybersecurity
How to become a security professional?
responses: Cybersecurity is the practice of protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks.
Security is built on the CIA Triad: Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability. Every security measure aims to protect one of these three pillars.
The main domains of security include Network Security, Application Security (AppSec), Cloud Security, and Incident Response.
Information Security (InfoSec) is the broad category of protecting data in any form, while Cybersecurity specifically focuses on protecting digital data and systems.

To start in security, you should learn the basics of Networking, Linux, and at least one programming language like Python, then move into specialized fields like Penetration Testing or Security Auditing.
Security is a continuous process, not a final product. It requires constant monitoring, patching, and learning about new threats.
MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication) is a security system that requires more than one method of authentication to verify the user's identity.
Common MFA methods include SMS codes, Authenticator apps (like Google Authenticator), and Biometrics (Fingerprint or FaceID).
Even if an attacker steals your password, they cannot access your account without the second factor, significantly reducing the risk of identity theft.
We highly recommend implementing MFA for all administrative and sensitive user accounts in your application.
Social Engineering is the art of manipulating people into giving up confidential information like passwords or bank details.
The most common type is 'Phishing', where attackers send fake emails or messages that look like they are from a trusted source.
Always check the sender's email address and never click on suspicious links or download attachments from unknown sources.
Security is not just about code; it's about people. Education and awareness are your best defenses against social engineering.

patterns: How to handle a data breach?
responses: Incident Response is the process of managing a security breach. The goal is to limit damage and reduce recovery time and costs.
The main steps are: Preparation, Identification, Containment, Eradication, Recovery, and Lessons Learned.
If you detect an attack, first 'Contain' it by isolating the affected systems to prevent the threat from spreading.
After recovering, always perform a 'Post-Mortem' analysis to understand how the breach happened and how to prevent it in the future.
Logging is the process of recording events happening in your application. Monitoring is the act of reviewing those logs to detect suspicious activities.
You should log all failed login attempts, password changes, and access to sensitive data to identify potential attacks early.
A good monitoring system can alert you in real-time if someone is trying to brute-force a user's password or perform an injection attack.
Logs are essential for forensic analysis after a breach occurs, helping you understand how the attacker got in and what they touched.
Cloud Security involves the technologies and policies used to protect data and infrastructure in cloud environments like AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud.
Understand the 'Shared Responsibility Model': The provider (e.g., AWS) secures the cloud hardware, but YOU are responsible for securing your data and application code.
Use IAM (Identity and Access Management) roles carefully in the cloud to ensure that services only have the permissions they need (Principle of Least Privilege).
Misconfigured cloud buckets (like S3) are a major cause of data leaks. Always ensure your cloud storage is not publicly accessible unless intended.
Red Teaming is the 'offensive' side; they act as attackers to find vulnerabilities. Blue Teaming is the 'defensive' side; they protect the system and respond to attacks.
Red Teams use penetration testing and social engineering to test the system's defenses. Blue Teams use logs, firewalls, and monitoring to stop them.
The goal of both teams is the same: to improve the organization's security through a simulated 'war game'.
A 'Purple Team' is when both Red and Blue teams work together to share knowledge and fix vulnerabilities more effectively.

patterns: How can an attacker exploit SSRF?
responses: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) is a vulnerability where an attacker forces the server to make requests to unintended locations, often internal services.
Attackers use SSRF to scan internal networks, access metadata services (like AWS metadata), or bypass firewalls that protect internal APIs.
To prevent SSRF, always use an 'Allow-list' of trusted domains and IP addresses that your server is allowed to communicate with.
Never allow users to provide full URLs for the server to fetch; instead, validate the destination and use internal IDs or predefined paths.
Disable unused URL schemas (like file://, gopher://, or ftp://) to limit what the server can request.
Broken Cryptography occurs when sensitive data is not properly protected, either by using weak algorithms or failing to encrypt data at rest and in transit.
Never use outdated algorithms like MD5, SHA1, or DES. Instead, use modern standards like AES-256 for encryption and SHA-256 for hashing.
Ensure all sensitive data is encrypted in transit using TLS (HTTPS). Data at rest in the database should also be encrypted using strong keys.
Key Management is crucial: never hardcode encryption keys in your code. Store them securely in a Key Management Service (KMS) or a Vault.
Avoid 'Security by Obscurity'; just because you created a custom encoding doesn't mean it's secure. Stick to proven, industry-standard cryptographic libraries.
Insecure Deserialization happens when an application converts malicious data back into an object, allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary code (RCE).
To prevent this, never deserialize data from untrusted sources without strict validation. Use safe data formats like JSON instead of complex serialized objects.
If you must use serialization, implement digital signatures or HMACs to ensure the data hasn't been tampered with before it's processed.
Run the deserialization code in low-privilege environments to limit the impact if an exploit occurs.
XXE (XML External Entity) is an attack against an application that parses XML input, allowing an attacker to view files on the server or interact with internal systems.
The best way to prevent XXE is to disable DTDs (Document Type Definitions) and external entities in your XML parser settings.
If you must use XML, use a safe library or switch to a less complex data format like JSON which is not vulnerable to XXE.
XXE can lead to sensitive data exposure, SSRF, and even Denial of Service (DoS) attacks.
Security Misconfiguration happens when security settings are not properly defined or implemented, such as keeping default passwords or leaving unnecessary features enabled.
To prevent this, always change default credentials, disable directory listing, and ensure that detailed error messages (Debug Info) are never shown to the user.
Regularly update and patch your software, frameworks, and servers to close known security holes.
A secure configuration involves hardening your environment by removing any unused tools or services that could be exploited.
IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) is a vulnerability where a user can access unauthorized data by simply changing a value (like an ID) in a URL or API request.
To prevent IDOR, never trust user-supplied IDs. Always check if the current user has the permission to access the specific object they are requesting.
Use non-predictable identifiers like UUIDs instead of sequential numbers (1, 2, 3) to make it harder for attackers to guess other object IDs.
Always implement robust server-side access control checks for every single request that accesses a database object.

patterns: How to check for security vulnerabilities in packages?
Risks of using third party libraries
responses: Vulnerable Components happen when you use third-party libraries or frameworks that have known security flaws.
Always keep your dependencies up to date. Use tools like 'npm audit' for Node.js or 'Safety' for Python to scan for vulnerable packages.
Remove any unused dependencies, features, and files from your project to reduce the attack surface.
Before adding a new library, check its security track record and how frequently it is patched by the maintainers.

patterns: How to know if my server is under attack?
responses: Insufficient Logging and Monitoring is when an application fails to record or alert on suspicious activities, allowing attackers to stay in the system longer.
Without proper logs, it is impossible to perform a forensic investigation after a breach to know what data was stolen.
Ensure that all login failures, high-value transactions, and server-side errors are logged with enough detail (timestamp, user ID, IP).
Set up real-time alerts for critical security events so your team can respond to an incident before it's too late.

patterns: What is a business logic vulnerability?
responses: Business Logic vulnerabilities are flaws in the design and implementation of an application that allow attackers to manipulate the business rules.
Common examples include bypassing a payment step, changing item quantities to negative numbers, or reusing a one-time discount code.
To prevent these, always validate the state and logic on the server-side. Never rely on the frontend to enforce business rules.
Think like an attacker: ask yourself, 'What happens if I skip this step?' or 'Can I submit this form with invalid data?' during the design phase.

patterns: Is it okay to put API keys in code?
responses: Never hardcode secrets like API keys, passwords, or tokens directly in your source code. If you push this code to a public repository, anyone can steal them.
Use environment variables (.env files) or dedicated Secret Management tools like HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager to store sensitive info.
Always add your config files (like .env) to your .gitignore file to prevent them from being uploaded to GitHub.
If you accidentally leak a secret, revoke it immediately and generate a new one. Once a secret is in the commit history, it is considered compromised.
Mobile security focuses on risks like insecure data storage on the device, weak biometric auth, and lack of code obfuscation.
IoT security is critical because many devices lack updates and use hardcoded default passwords, making them easy targets for Botnets.
For mobile apps, always use 'Certificate Pinning' to prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks and encrypt local databases like SQLite.
Security principles remain the same: Validate input, use strong encryption, and follow the Principle of Least Privilege across all platforms.
AI Security involves protecting Machine Learning models from attacks like 'Prompt Injection', where a user tricks the AI into ignoring its safety rules.
Data Poisoning is another risk, where an attacker manipulates the training data to create a 'backdoor' in the AI model.
To secure AI applications, never give the AI direct access to sensitive APIs without human-in-the-loop verification.
Sanitize the inputs sent to LLMs just like you sanitize SQL queries to prevent the model from executing unintended instructions.
XXE (XML External Entity) occurs when an application parses XML input and allows external entities, potentially exposing server files.
To prevent XXE, disable DTDs (Document Type Definitions) in your XML parser.
Switching to JSON instead of XML is a modern way to avoid XXE vulnerabilities entirely.
IDOR happens when a user can access unauthorized resources by changing an ID in a URL (e.g., changing /user/10 to /user/11).
Always perform server-side access control checks to ensure the user owns the data they are requesting.
Using UUIDs (random strings) instead of sequential numbers makes it much harder for attackers to guess IDs.
Security Misconfiguration is leaving default settings, unused features, or debug modes enabled in production.
Always change default passwords and disable directory listing on your web server.
Ensure error messages don't leak technical details (like stack traces) to the end user.
Using UUIDs (random strings) instead of sequential numbers makes it much harder for attackers to guess object IDs.
Session Hijacking occurs when an attacker steals a user's session ID. To prevent this, always use HTTPS and set the 'Secure' and 'HttpOnly' flags on cookies.
Always implement session timeouts. If a user is inactive, the session should automatically expire to protect their account.
Regenerate the Session ID after every login to prevent Session Fixation attacks.
Security Headers are instructions from the server to the browser to enable security features like CSP (Content Security Policy).
To prevent 'Clickjacking', use the 'X-Frame-Options: DENY' header to stop your site from being loaded in an iframe.
HSTS ensures that the browser only communicates with your server over encrypted HTTPS, never plain HTTP.

patterns: How to handle user data privacy?
responses: PII (Personally Identifiable Information) includes data like emails and IDs. Always encrypt this data in your database.
Apply 'Data Minimization': only collect the information you absolutely need to run your service.
Privacy by Design means building security and privacy into your project from the very first step, not as an afterthought.
Insecure File Upload occurs when an application allows users to upload malicious files (like scripts) to the server.
To prevent this, always validate file extensions using an 'Allow-list' (e.g., .jpg, .png only) and never rely on the MIME type provided by the user.
Store uploaded files in a separate directory outside the web root and rename them to random strings to prevent execution.
Use a virus scanner to check every uploaded file before it is permanently stored on your server.
SSTI occurs when user input is unsafely embedded into a web template, allowing attackers to execute code on the server.
To prevent SSTI, never pass user-provided data directly into template strings. Use the template engine's built-in filtering and escaping.
Treat template code as executable code; always use a 'Sandbox' environment if you must allow dynamic templates.

patterns: What is a Race Condition in web?
responses: A Race Condition happens when multiple requests try to access and modify the same data at the exact same time (e.g., spending the same balance twice).
To prevent this, use 'Database Locking' or 'Atomic Operations' to ensure that only one process can modify a record at a time.
Always validate the state of the data (like checking if a balance is sufficient) immediately before performing an update.

patterns: Give me a secure coding checklist
How to review my code for security?
responses: Here is your Secure Coding Checklist: 1. Validate all inputs. 2. Use Parameterized Queries for DB. 3. Encrypt sensitive data. 4. Use Secure Cookies (HttpOnly/Secure). 5. Keep dependencies updated. 6. Never hardcode secrets.
Before deploying, ensure: You have no hardcoded API keys, error messages don't leak server info, and you use strong hashing (like BCrypt) for passwords.
Check these 3 things: Authentication (Who are you?), Authorization (What can you do?), and Auditing (What did you do?).

patterns: What tools should I use for security?
responses: For Static Analysis (SAST), use tools like SonarQube or Snyk. For Dynamic Analysis (DAST), OWASP ZAP and Burp Suite are industry standards.
To check for vulnerable dependencies, use 'npm audit' for Node.js, 'Safety' for Python, or GitHub's Dependabot.
If you want to scan your web app for common flaws, OWASP ZAP is a great free and open-source tool to start with.
Clickjacking is an attack that tricks a user into clicking something different from what they perceive, usually by using invisible iframes.
The best defense is using the 'X-Frame-Options' HTTP header set to 'DENY' or 'SAMEORIGIN'.
Modern browsers also support the 'Content-Security-Policy' (CSP) with 'frame-ancestors' directive to prevent your site from being embedded elsewhere.
Broken Access Control is when users can access resources or perform actions outside of their intended permissions.
Horizontal escalation is accessing data of users at the same level, while Vertical escalation is a regular user gaining Admin rights.
To prevent this, deny access by default and check permissions on the server-side for every single request.

patterns: How to protect cloud instances from SSRF?
responses: In cloud environments, SSRF can be used to query the Metadata service (169.254.169.254) to steal temporary security credentials.
Attackers can use these credentials to gain full access to your S3 buckets or other cloud resources.
Always use IMDSv2 (for AWS) which requires a session token, making it much harder to exploit via SSRF.
BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization) is the most common API threat. It's when an API endpoint doesn't check if the user has access to the requested ID.
Even if you use random IDs like UUIDs, you MUST still verify that the user 'owns' the resource they are trying to access.
Implement a centralized authorization mechanism to validate access for every API call.
Directory Traversal allows attackers to read arbitrary files on the server by using '../' sequences in file paths.
To prevent this, never pass user input directly to file APIs. Use a hardcoded list of allowed files or sanitize the input to remove dots and slashes.
Run your web server with the lowest possible privileges so it can't access sensitive system directories.
Open Redirection occurs when an application uses a user-supplied URL to redirect them without validation (e.g., ?redirect=http://evil.com).
Attackers use this to make their phishing links look trustworthy because they start with your legitimate domain.
Avoid using user input for redirects. If you must, use an allow-list of trusted local URLs or internal IDs.
Mass Assignment happens when a framework automatically binds user input to a database model without filtering the fields.
An attacker can exploit this to change fields they shouldn't touch, like 'role', 'balance', or 'admin_status'.
Always use 'Data Transfer Objects' (DTOs) or explicit allow-lists to define exactly which fields the user is allowed to update.
Rate Limiting restricts the number of requests a user can make in a given time, preventing Brute Force and Denial of Service (DoS) attacks.
Implement 'Account Lockout' or increasing delays after multiple failed login attempts to stop automated password guessing.
Use tools like Redis or middleware in Express/Flask to track and limit requests per IP address.
Out-of-Band (OOB) XXE is used when the application doesn't return the XML response. The attacker tricks the server into sending data to their own external server.
This is often done using 'External Parameter Entities' to wrap system files inside a request to the attacker's URL.
To prevent all forms of XXE, the golden rule is: Disable DTDs and External Entities in your XML parsing library configuration.
HPP occurs when an application receives multiple parameters with the same name and processes them inconsistently, which can bypass security filters.
For example, a WAF might check the first 'id', while the application logic uses the second one.
Always validate that your application handles repeated parameters predictably and use frameworks that let you access parameters strictly by name.

patterns: Using iframe in PDF to steal data
responses: Many PDF generators render HTML/JS. An attacker can use tags like <iframe> or <script> to make the server fetch internal files or hit internal APIs.
If you use libraries like wkhtmltopdf, ensure that access to local files (file://) and external network requests is disabled.
Always sanitize the HTML input before passing it to a PDF rendering engine to prevent unauthorized server-side requests.
Prototype Pollution is a JavaScript vulnerability where an attacker can add properties to global object prototypes, leading to RCE or Logic bypass.
It often happens during 'Deep Merge' or 'Property Assignment' operations on JSON data.
Use 'Map' instead of plain objects for user-controlled keys, or use 'Object.freeze()' to protect your prototypes.
GraphQL often has 'Introspection' enabled by default, which lets attackers see the entire schema, including hidden fields and queries.
Attackers can also perform 'Query Depth' attacks, sending deeply nested queries that crash the server (DoS).
Disable introspection in production and implement 'Query Cost Analysis' or 'Depth Limiting' to protect your GraphQL server.

patterns: How to prevent host header attacks?
responses: Host Header Injection happens when a server uses the 'Host' header to generate absolute URLs (like in emails) without validation.
An attacker can change the Host to 'evil.com', making the application send a password reset link that points to the attacker's server.
Always use a hardcoded domain name for generating URLs in emails and validate the Host header against a trusted allow-list.
Subdomain Takeover occurs when a DNS record points to a service that has been deleted. An attacker can claim that service and host their own content on your subdomain.
This allows attackers to bypass CSP, steal session cookies, and launch very convincing phishing attacks.
Regularly audit your DNS records and remove any that point to services you no longer use (Dangling DNS).
Goodbye! Keep your code safe.
See you later! Happy secure coding.
Bye! Don't forget to validate your inputs!
